text.title: Page Object Pattern Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every page object in a Playwright or Cypress suite must satisfy. Hard rules; locator-audit hook rejects PRs that violate them.

rule.statement: Locator priority is strict: role-based (getByRole, findByRole) FIRST, then test-id (getByTestId, data-testid), then text (getByText), and ONLY as last resort CSS / XPath. No page object mixes priorities for the same element. A locator's choice must be commented with the next-best alternative if the chosen one is not role-based.

rationale.source: Kent C. Dodds testing-library priority; Playwright official locator priority guidance

rule.rationale: Role-based locators survive design refactors that change CSS classes and DOM structure. CSS / XPath locators break the moment a designer renames a class. Mixing locator types within a page object guarantees inconsistent breakage when the UI evolves. The strict priority order is the single highest-leverage discipline for a maintainable E2E suite.

example.title: Strict locator priority
Mixed locators
example.note: Mixed priorities mean the page object breaks at four different change-triggers (id change, name change, button text change, DOM-structure change). The locator-audit hook rejects this file.

rule.statement: Public methods on a page object express user-meaningful actions, not DOM interactions. `loginAs(user)` is allowed; `typeUsername`, `typePassword`, `clickSubmit` are not allowed as public methods. The page object's public surface is the user's mental model, not the developer's interaction model.

rationale.source: Selenium and Playwright Page Object best practice; testing-library philosophy

rule.rationale: When tests call `typeUsername` then `typePassword` then `clickSubmit`, a UI refactor that combines email and password into a single SSO button breaks 30 tests. When tests call `loginAs(user)`, the same refactor changes ONE method on the page object and the 30 tests keep passing. The user-action level of abstraction is what survives UI redesigns.

rule.statement: No implicit waits, no fixed-time waits. Forbidden: `page.waitForTimeout`, `cy.wait(2000)`, sleep(). Allowed: `expect(locator).toBeVisible()`, `await locator.waitFor({ state: 'visible' })`, `cy.findByRole(...).should('be.visible')`. All waits are on a condition, never on the clock.

rationale.source: Playwright auto-waiting documentation; flaky-test root cause analysis

rule.rationale: Time-based waits are the single biggest source of E2E flakiness. Either they are too short on a slow CI and break randomly, or they are too long and the suite becomes painfully slow. Condition-based waits succeed as fast as possible and fail meaningfully when the condition never holds.

rule.statement: Every page object has a single contract test that exercises its public surface against a known-good snapshot (smoke navigation, key locator visibility, key user actions complete). When a page-object PR changes a locator or method signature, the contract test fails in THAT PR — selector drift is caught at the page-object author, not at 30 downstream test authors.

rationale.source: contract-testing patterns from consumer-contract testing; cost-of-defect curves

rule.rationale: Selector breakage discovered in 30 unrelated PRs is the most expensive way to find it. A per-page contract test moves the failure to the PR that introduced the change, where the cost-to-fix is lowest. The contract test runs in < 30 seconds per page; the team-wide cost saved is enormous.

rule.statement: Tests MUST NOT access the underlying `page` / `cy` object directly when a page object exists for the URL. Lint rule: in `tests/*.spec.ts`, direct calls to `page.locator`, `page.click`, `cy.get` outside a page object's methods are flagged.
rule.rationale: Direct DOM access from tests defeats the abstraction. Every direct access is a future selector-rot hotspot that the contract test cannot protect. Encapsulation discipline is the only way the page-object pattern actually pays off.

list.item: pages/ — one file per page or per meaningful component (login.page.ts, dashboard.page.ts)
pages/base/ — BasePage class with the locator-priority helpers and explicit-wait helpers
pages/contracts/ — per-page contract test (one per page object)
tests/ — test files that use page objects exclusively
fixtures/ — test data, user roles, no DOM helpers
